your_representative.name: N/A — Irvine is a neighboring city, not primary jurisdiction
your_representative.note: Recently restructured: 6 district seats + elected mayor. 5-2 Democratic majority. 3 seats + mayor on Nov 2026 ballot. Key issue: General Plan (adopted Aug 2024, 57,656 units). APRIL 14: City Council APPROVED zoning text amendment enabling alternative open space framework for Planning Area 12 (Oak Creek) — the 50-acre nature park can now replace the golf course as open space. Broader project approvals for the 3,100-unit development still pending. Opposition gathering ~19,000 signatures for ballot initiative. Irvine Company monopoly backdrop.

governing_bodies.name: Orange County Board of Supervisors

your_representative.name: Vicente Sarmiento (D) or Donald Wagner (R)
your_representative.district: District 2 or 3 (near boundary — depends on exact address)
your_representative.note: Currently 3-2 Democratic majority. CRITICAL: District 4 four-way primary (Jung $354K, Traut $225K, Espinoza $164K, Shaw $82K). District 5: incumbent Foley (D, $285K) vs Assemblywoman Dixon (R, $135K). ⚡ April 23 finance filing deadline in 4 DAYS — new fundraising data imminent. 44 days to June 2 primary. If Democrats lose either seat, board flips to Republican majority. Board controls $10.8B annual budget.

governing_bodies.name: OCTA (Orange County Transportation Authority)
governing_bodies.note: OC Streetcar 92-95% complete, in active street testing since Feb 20. OC's first modern rail transit line. Metrolink commuter rail also serves the area.

upcoming_elections.race: OC Board of Supervisors District 4 — Open Seat (Chaffee termed out)
candidates: Fred Jung (Fullerton Mayor, $354K cash on hand — dominant fundraiser)
Connor Traut (Buena Park Vice Mayor, $225K, OC Dem Party + Chaffee endorsement)

Jung's $354K and Traut's $225K + party endorsement make them likely top-two. Filing closed March 6. Next finance filing April 23.
upcoming_elections.action: Research all four candidates on housing, transit, homelessness, and budget positions. Jung and Traut are the fundraising leaders. Consider donations and volunteering for preferred candidate.

bridge_building.why_this_race_matters_to_everyone: This contest influences service delivery, affordability, and institutional trust for residents well beyond partisan bases.
bridge_building.shared_stakes: Most voters want competent governance, transparent budgeting, and practical progress on housing, transportation, and public safety.
bridge_building.anti_tribal_framing: Use a performance lens: compare who can execute durable solutions and communicate tradeoffs honestly, rather than treating the race as team identity warfare.

upcoming_elections.race: OC Board of Supervisors District 5 — Foley (D) vs Dixon (R)
candidates: Katrina Foley (D, incumbent, $285K cash, Board Vice Chair)
Diane Dixon (R, Assemblywoman, $135K cash, outraised Foley in H2 2025)
upcoming_elections.relevance: CRITICAL: Most vulnerable Democratic seat. District is most Republican of the 5 (38.1% R vs 32.8% D). Foley won 51-49 in 2022. Harris won D5 by just 318 votes (49-48%). If Dixon wins, board flips regardless of District 4 outcome. Arguably the most consequential OC race.
upcoming_elections.action: Research both candidates. Support Foley if aligned with your priorities. Dixon outraised Foley in H2 2025 — financial momentum matters. This is the defensive counterpart to District 4's offensive opportunity.

SECOND DEBATE at Pomona College April 28 (CBS broadcast). Democratic fragmentation WORSE than ever: 4-way D split vs 2-way R split. R-vs-R November most realistic path. Swalwell's zombie candidacy stays on June 2 ballot. Early ballots going out in ~3 weeks. Housing is #1 voter issue (20%). Governor controls RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals — upstream of every OC housing fight.
upcoming_elections.action: WATCH April 22 debate (7pm, KTLA5) — 3 DAYS AWAY. Then Pomona College debate April 28 (CBS). Vote strategically in top-two primary — Dem fragmentation could produce R-R November. Research which D candidate is strongest on housing enforcement. The next governor determines whether the OC housing fight has a state ally.

key_political_dynamics:
Orange County is one of America's few truly purple counties. The area around UCI Medical Center sits at the exact fulcrum — the City of Orange is split 36.6% D to 34.8% R. Elections here are decided by razor margins (the 2024 CD-45 race was decided by 581 votes). California's state housing mandates (SB 9, RHNA, Builder's Remedy) create intense friction with local governments that resist density. The 2026 elections are unusually high-stakes: the Board of Supervisors majority, multiple open state legislative seats, city council races in both Orange and Irvine, and new congressional maps from Prop 50 all converge. For a moderate Democrat, this is the most target-rich political environment in America.

April 2026 updates: (1) GOVERNOR'S RACE — DEBATE WEEK: Debate lineup CONFIRMED — 6 candidates on stage April 22 (3 DAYS AWAY, 7pm, KTLA5/Nexstar): Hilton (R, 17%), Bianco (R, 14%), Steyer (D, 14%), Becerra (D, 10%), Porter (D, 10%), Mahan (D, 5%). SECOND DEBATE at Pomona College April 28 (CBS broadcast). Democratic fragmentation worsening. R-vs-R November most realistic path. Early ballots ~3 weeks out. (2) ⚡ APRIL 23 CAMPAIGN FINANCE DEADLINE in 4 DAYS: New fundraising data for BoS D4/D5, state legislative races, and governor's race. Reveals spending trajectories for final 6 weeks. 44 days until June 2 primary. (3) IRVINE OAK CREEK MILESTONE: City Council APPROVED zoning text amendment on April 14 enabling alternative open space framework for Planning Area 12 — the 50-acre nature park can now substitute for golf course open space. This is a critical procedural step. Broader project approvals for the 3,100-unit development ($96M community benefits, 2,000 rent vouchers, 50-acre nature park) still pending. Opposition gathering signatures for ballot initiative (~19,000 needed). (4) SD-34: Valencia (D) vs Shader (R) — straight D-vs-R race. Newman NOT running. (5) AD-68 fundraising: Peñaloza $142,897 vs Lopez $73,737 — nearly 2x gap. (6) CD-40: Prop 50 redistricting pits Young Kim (R, $2M+ cash) vs Ken Calvert (R). 10 candidates total. (7) OC Streetcar: Launch target revised to AUGUST 2026 (from 'spring'). 92-95% complete, street testing continues. (8) Post-Grants Pass enforcement escalation: anti-camping ordinance passed Nov 2025, residents serving jail time. Grand Jury recommended prioritizing prevention over reaction by June 30. (9) 2026 PIT Count conducted Jan 26-29 — results not yet published.

Prior March context: BoS D4 Jung $354K, Traut $225K. D5 Dixon outraised Foley. City of Orange achieved Housing Element compliance but 696 Builder's Remedy units pending. Newsom warned 15 noncompliant cities.

Key difference from Austin #1: Purple county in a blue state (vs blue city in red state). OC voter registration: 36.4% D, 33.9% R, 23.3% NPP. Doug's vote carries MORE weight in local races than in Austin. Elections here are decided by hundreds of votes, not thousands.

Key difference #2: California's state framework is an ally, not an enemy. In Texas, the state legislature actively preempts Austin's progressive policies. In California, the state government MANDATES progressive housing policy (SB 9, SB 10, RHNA, Builder's Remedy) that local governments resist. The YIMBY fight flips: instead of pushing local government to be more progressive, you're pushing local government to comply with state mandates it's trying to evade. Newsom's March 25 final warning to 15 noncompliant cities — with AG referral threatened — reinforces the enforcement posture.

Key difference #3: The Irvine Company monopoly. Irvine Company (Donald Bren, America's richest real estate developer) controls ~75% of Irvine's apartment units and much of the commercial real estate. Their proposed 3,100-unit Oak Creek Golf Course conversion — with $96M in community benefits, 2,000 rent vouchers, and a 50-acre nature park — is the first major implementation test of the General Plan. Planning Commission unanimously approved it. This is a unique housing dynamic — one private entity has near-monopoly power over rents and development in a city of 314,000 people.

immediate.action: Watch Governor's debate — April 22, 7pm — 3 DAYS AWAY
immediate.detail: Debate lineup CONFIRMED: 6 candidates — Hilton (R), Bianco (R), Steyer (D), Becerra (D), Porter (D), Mahan (D). Watch on KTLA5/Nexstar stations. SECOND debate at Pomona College April 28 (CBS broadcast). Evaluate each candidate on housing enforcement: RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals. Democratic fragmentation is the key strategic question — 4-way D split risks R-vs-R November.
immediate.reason: The governor controls RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy, and AG referrals — upstream of every OC housing fight. Two debates in one week. Need to decide which Democrat to support before early ballots arrive in ~3 weeks.
immediate.llm_prompt: I'm watching TWO California Governor's debates: April 22 (KTLA5/Nexstar, 7pm) and April 28 (Pomona College/CBS). Six confirmed candidates: Steve Hilton (R, 17%), Chad Bianco (R, 14%), Tom Steyer (D, 14%), Xavier Becerra (D, 10% — surging), Katie Porter (D, 10%), Matt Mahan (D, 5%). I'm a pro-housing YIMBY in OC. Help me prepare: (1) What housing enforcement questions should I listen for? (2) How do I evaluate each candidate's RHNA/Builder's Remedy positions? (3) How should I think about strategic voting in the top-two primary given the 4-way Democratic split? The risk is R-vs-R in November. (4) Compare Becerra's HHS enforcement background vs Porter's consumer protection background vs Steyer's investor perspective vs Mahan's mayor experience on housing policy.

immediate.action: Track April 23 campaign finance filings — 4 DAYS AWAY
immediate.detail: First required pre-election 460 filing covers 1/1/26-4/18/26. New fundraising data for BoS D4/D5 races, AD-68, SD-34, governor's race. Prior data: Jung $354K, Traut $225K, Foley $285K, Dixon $135K. Updated figures will show spending rates and donor trajectories heading into the final 6 weeks before June 2 primary.
immediate.reason: Finance data reveals which candidates have momentum and resources for the final stretch. 44 days to primary.

immediate.detail: registertovote.ca.gov — update registration with new OC address as soon as you have one
immediate.reason: Must be registered to vote in June 2026 primary (registration deadline ~May 18)
immediate.llm_prompt: I'm moving from Austin, TX to Orange, CA (92868) and need to register to vote in California. Walk me through the process at registertovote.ca.gov. What are the key deadlines for the June 2, 2026 primary? Can I register online or do I need to do it in person? What ID do I need? Also explain California's top-two primary system and how it differs from Texas primaries.

strategic.llm_prompt: I'm a YIMBY moving from Austin, TX to Orange County, CA. In Austin, the YIMBY ecosystem is well-developed (AURA, Austin YIMBY Action, etc.). OC seems thinner — People for Housing OC has only 2 staffers. Help me map the OC housing advocacy ecosystem: who are the key organizations, what are their focuses, where are the gaps? How does California YIMBY (state-level) interact with local OC groups? What role does the Kennedy Commission play? Is there a need for new organizing, or should I plug into existing groups?

strategic.detail: Maintain Austin connections (District 1 race, AURA) while building OC engagement. Target: 1 hour/week per location.
strategic.reason: The dual-location model only works if both locations get consistent attention
